title: SLOs, SLIs, and Error Budgets: A Practical Implementation Guide
description: A step-by-step checklist for defining service level objectives, picking the right service level indicators, and using error budgets to make better decisions about reliability vs. feature velocity.

items.title: Identify your critical user journeys
items.description: Before you touch any metrics, figure out what your users actually care about. A checkout flow matters more than an admin settings page. If you skip this step, you'll end up with SLOs that protect things nobody uses while ignoring the paths that make or break your business.

codeBlocks.label: Document critical user journeys
codeBlocks.code:
# critical-journeys.yaml
service: payment-api
journeys:
  - name: checkout
    description: "User completes a purchase"
    priority: P0
    endpoints:
      - POST /api/v1/orders
      - POST /api/v1/payments
      - GET /api/v1/orders/{id}/status
    expected_latency_p99: 800ms
    expected_availability: 99.95%

  - name: product-search
    description: "User searches and browses products"
    priority: P1
    endpoints:
      - GET /api/v1/search
      - GET /api/v1/products/{id}
    expected_latency_p99: 200ms
    expected_availability: 99.9%

  - name: admin-reports
    description: "Internal team generates reports"
    priority: P2
    endpoints:
      - GET /api/v1/admin/reports
    expected_latency_p99: 5000ms
    expected_availability: 99.5%

items.title: Pick SLIs that reflect real user experience
items.description: Your SLIs should measure what users feel, not what your servers report. A health check returning 200 means nothing if the checkout page takes 12 seconds to load. Stick to the four golden signals: latency, error rate, throughput, and saturation. Measure at the edge, not deep inside your stack.

codeBlocks.label: SLI: Availability (success rate at the load balancer)
codeBlocks.code:
# Good: measures what users see at the edge
sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="api-gateway", status!~"5.."}[5m]))
/
sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="api-gateway"}[5m]))

# Bad: internal health check (tells you nothing about user experience)
# up{job="api-gateway"} == 1

codeBlocks.label: SLI: Latency (p99 request duration)
codeBlocks.code:
# Percentage of requests faster than 300ms
sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{le="0.3", job="api-gateway"}[5m]))
/
sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_count{job="api-gateway"}[5m]))

codeBlocks.label: SLI: Correctness (application-level errors)
codeBlocks.code:
# Catches business logic errors, not just HTTP 500s
1 - (
  sum(rate(app_errors_total{job="api-gateway", type=~"validation|timeout|upstream"}[5m]))
  /
  sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="api-gateway"}[5m]))
)

items.title: Set SLO targets based on actual data, not wishful thinking
items.description: Run your service for 2-4 weeks and look at the real numbers before setting targets. If your current availability is 99.5%, setting a 99.99% SLO is a fantasy. Start with what you can actually hit, then tighten over time. An SLO you consistently miss is worse than useless -- it trains everyone to ignore alerts.

codeBlocks.label: Query historical performance to inform SLO targets
codeBlocks.code:
# Check your actual availability over the last 30 days
curl -s 'http://prometheus:9090/api/v1/query' \
  --data-urlencode 'query=
    avg_over_time(
      (sum(rate(http_requests_total{status!~"5.."}[1h]))
       / sum(rate(http_requests_total[1h])))[30d:1h]
    )' | jq '.data.result[0].value[1]'

# Output: "0.9987"  <- your real availability is 99.87%
# Don't set an SLO of 99.99% if this is where you are

codeBlocks.label: SLO definition based on measured performance
codeBlocks.code:
# slo-spec.yaml
apiVersion: sloth.slok.dev/v1
kind: PrometheusServiceLevel
metadata:
  name: payment-api
spec:
  service: payment-api
  labels:
    team: platform
  slos:
    - name: requests-availability
      objective: 99.9  # 43.8 min/month downtime budget
      description: "99.9% of payment requests succeed"
      sli:
        events:
          error_query: sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="payment-api", status=~"5.."}[{{.window}}]))
          total_query: sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="payment-api"}[{{.window}}]))
      alerting:
        name: PaymentAvailability
        labels:
          severity: critical
        page_alert:
          labels:
            severity: page
        ticket_alert:
          labels:
            severity: ticket

items.title: Calculate and track your error budget
items.description: An error budget is the amount of unreliability you can afford. With a 99.9% SLO, you get 0.1% of requests as your budget. This is not a target to hit -- it is the line where you stop shipping features and start fixing reliability. Without a visible error budget, the conversation about reliability vs. speed never has numbers behind it.

codeBlocks.label: Error budget remaining (30-day rolling window)
codeBlocks.code:
# Error budget consumption percentage
# SLO target: 99.9% (0.999)
(
  1 - (
    sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="payment-api", status!~"5.."}[30d]))
    /
    sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="payment-api"}[30d]))
  )
) / (1 - 0.999) * 100

# Result interpretation:
# 0-70%   = healthy, keep shipping
# 70-90%  = slow down, prioritize reliability work
# 90-100% = freeze feature deployments
# >100%   = SLO violated, all hands on reliability

codeBlocks.label: Quick error budget math
codeBlocks.code:
# Monthly error budget in minutes and requests
# 99.9% SLO over 30 days:
# Time budget:    30 * 24 * 60 * 0.001 = 43.2 minutes of downtime
# Request budget: 1,000,000 requests/month * 0.001 = 1,000 failed requests allowed

echo "SLO: 99.9%"
echo "Monthly downtime budget: $(echo '30 * 24 * 60 * 0.001' | bc) minutes"
echo "At 1M requests/month: $(echo '1000000 * 0.001' | bc | cut -d. -f1) failed requests allowed"

# Output:
# SLO: 99.9%
# Monthly downtime budget: 43.2 minutes
# At 1M requests/month: 1000 failed requests allowed

items.title: Set up burn rate alerts instead of threshold alerts
items.description: Don't alert when availability drops below 99.9% in a 5-minute window. That fires constantly and means nothing. Use burn rate alerts instead: they tell you how fast you're consuming your error budget. A 14.4x burn rate means you'll exhaust your monthly budget in 2 days. That's worth waking someone up for. A 1x burn rate means you're on track -- no action needed.

codeBlocks.label: Multi-window burn rate alerting rules
codeBlocks.code:
# prometheus-rules.yaml
groups:
  - name: slo-burn-rate
    rules:
      # Fast burn: 2% of budget in 1 hour -> pages oncall
      - alert: HighErrorBudgetBurn_Page
        expr: |
          (
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="payment-api", status=~"5.."}[1h]))
            / sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="payment-api"}[1h]))
          ) > (14.4 * 0.001)
          and
          (
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="payment-api", status=~"5.."}[5m]))
            / sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="payment-api"}[5m]))
          ) > (14.4 * 0.001)
        for: 2m
        labels:
          severity: page
        annotations:
          summary: "Payment API burning error budget 14.4x faster than allowed"
          description: "At this rate, the 30-day error budget will be exhausted in ~2 days."
          runbook: "https://wiki.internal/runbooks/payment-api-slo-burn"

      # Slow burn: 10% of budget in 3 days -> creates a ticket
      - alert: HighErrorBudgetBurn_Ticket
        expr: |
          (
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="payment-api", status=~"5.."}[6h]))
            / sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="payment-api"}[6h]))
          ) > (3 * 0.001)
          and
          (
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="payment-api", status=~"5.."}[3h]))
            / sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="payment-api"}[3h]))
          ) > (3 * 0.001)
        for: 15m
        labels:
          severity: ticket
        annotations:
          summary: "Payment API burning error budget 3x faster than allowed"
          description: "At this rate, the 30-day error budget will be exhausted in ~10 days."

items.title: Build an error budget dashboard everyone can see
items.description: Your error budget is useless if only the SRE team knows about it. Put it on a dashboard that product managers and engineering leads check weekly. When the budget is running low, the conversation about whether to ship that risky migration or fix that flaky retry logic happens naturally.

codeBlocks.label: Grafana dashboard panel for error budget

items.title: Write an error budget policy and get sign-off
items.description: An error budget without a policy is just a number on a dashboard. You need written rules that say what happens when the budget runs low or runs out. Does deployment freeze? Do engineers switch to reliability work? Who decides when to override the freeze? Get your VP of Engineering and product lead to sign this. If nobody agreed to follow the rules, nobody will.

codeBlocks.label: Error budget policy document
codeBlocks.code:
# error-budget-policy.yaml
service: payment-api
slo_target: 99.9%
window: 30d
approved_by:
  - VP Engineering
  - Director of Product
  - SRE Lead

policy:
  budget_healthy:  # 0-70% consumed
    actions:
      - "Normal feature development continues"
      - "Standard deployment cadence"
      - "SRE reviews weekly burn rate in standup"

  budget_warning:  # 70-90% consumed
    actions:
      - "Risky deployments require SRE approval"
      - "Team prioritizes one reliability item per sprint"
      - "Daily error budget check in standup"
      - "No new experimental features"

  budget_critical:  # 90-100% consumed
    actions:
      - "Feature freeze -- only bug fixes and reliability work"
      - "All deployments require SRE sign-off"
      - "Rollback plan required for every change"
      - "Incident review for top error contributors"

  budget_exhausted:  # >100% consumed
    actions:
      - "Full deployment freeze"
      - "All engineering effort on reliability"
      - "Executive escalation within 24 hours"
      - "Post-mortem required before freeze lifts"

  override_conditions:
    - "Security patches always ship regardless of budget"
    - "Legal/compliance changes always ship"
    - "VP Engineering can override freeze with written justification"

items.title: Instrument your code to emit SLI metrics
items.description: Your SLIs are only as good as the data feeding them. Add instrumentation at the HTTP layer and the application layer. HTTP status codes miss a lot -- a 200 response with an empty cart is still a failure for the user. Track both infrastructure-level and business-level signals.

codeBlocks.label: Flask app with SLI instrumentation

items.title: Use rolling windows, not calendar windows
items.description: A calendar-month SLO resets on the 1st. That means your team blows through the budget on the 3rd and then coasts for 27 days knowing nothing matters until next month. Use a 30-day rolling window instead. Every hour matters equally. Yesterday's incident is always in the window.

codeBlocks.label: Rolling 30-day window SLO calculation
codeBlocks.code:
# Rolling 30-day availability (every hour counts)
sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="payment-api", status!~"5.."}[30d]))
/
sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="payment-api"}[30d]))

# Compare: calendar month (resets, creates bad incentives)
# sum(increase(http_requests_total{job="payment-api", status!~"5.."}[30d]))
# This resets on the 1st -- avoid this pattern

codeBlocks.label: Prometheus recording rules for SLO windows
codeBlocks.code:
# Recording rules to pre-compute SLO values
# Needed because 30d range queries are expensive
groups:
  - name: slo-recording-rules
    interval: 1m
    rules:
      # 5m error rate (for burn rate alerts)
      - record: slo:http_error_rate:ratio_rate5m
        expr: |
          sum(rate(http_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[5m])) by (job)
          / sum(rate(http_requests_total[5m])) by (job)

      # 1h error rate
      - record: slo:http_error_rate:ratio_rate1h
        expr: |
          sum(rate(http_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[1h])) by (job)
          / sum(rate(http_requests_total[1h])) by (job)

      # 6h error rate
      - record: slo:http_error_rate:ratio_rate6h
        expr: |
          sum(rate(http_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[6h])) by (job)
          / sum(rate(http_requests_total[6h])) by (job)

      # 30d error rate (for overall SLO tracking)
      - record: slo:http_error_rate:ratio_rate30d
        expr: |
          sum(rate(http_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[30d])) by (job)
          / sum(rate(http_requests_total[30d])) by (job)

items.title: Automate weekly SLO status reports
items.description: Send a weekly SLO report to your team's Slack channel or email list. Include current SLO status, error budget remaining, top error contributors, and trending direction. This keeps SLOs visible without anyone needing to check a dashboard. Automation beats good intentions every time.

codeBlocks.label: Weekly SLO report script
Cron job for weekly SLO report
codeBlocks.code:
# Add to crontab: run every Monday at 9am UTC
0 9 * * 1 /usr/local/bin/python3 /opt/sre/slo-report.py >> /var/log/slo-report.log 2>&1

items.title: Define a clear response process for SLO violations
items.description: When your SLO is violated, you need a playbook, not a panic. Who gets paged? What's the first thing they check? When does it escalate? Write this down before the incident happens. During an outage is the worst time to figure out your process.

codeBlocks.label: SLO violation response playbook
codeBlocks.code:
# slo-violation-runbook.yaml
service: payment-api
slo: 99.9% availability

response_process:
  immediate:  # within 5 minutes of page
    - "Acknowledge the alert in PagerDuty"
    - "Check error budget dashboard: is this a spike or slow burn?"
    - "Run: kubectl get pods -n payment | grep -v Running"
    - "Check recent deployments: kubectl rollout history deploy/payment-api -n payment"
    - "If a deploy happened in last 2h, roll back first, investigate second"

  triage:  # within 15 minutes
    - "Identify top error contributor from logs"
    - "Check upstream dependencies (database, cache, third-party APIs)"
    - "Determine blast radius: all users or a segment?"
    - "Update #incidents channel with status"

  escalation:
    - condition: "Budget burn rate >14x for 15+ minutes"
      action: "Page secondary oncall and engineering lead"
    - condition: "Budget exhausted (>100% consumed)"
      action: "Page VP Engineering, initiate full incident response"
    - condition: "Customer-facing impact confirmed"
      action: "Notify support team, prepare status page update"

  post_violation:
    - "Schedule blameless post-mortem within 48 hours"
    - "Identify and assign reliability action items"
    - "Review SLO target: was it realistic?"
    - "Update runbook with lessons learned"

items.title: Keep your SLOs tighter than your SLAs
items.description: Your SLA is a contract with customers. Your SLO is your internal target. Always set your SLO higher than your SLA. If your SLA promises 99.9%, set your SLO at 99.95%. This gives you a buffer zone. You'll know you're in trouble before your customers do, and before you owe anyone credits.

codeBlocks.label: SLA vs SLO gap configuration
codeBlocks.code:
# The SLO-SLA gap gives you early warning
service: payment-api

sla:  # what you promise customers (contractual)
  availability: 99.9%
  latency_p95: 1000ms
  penalty: "10% credit per 0.1% below SLA"

slo:  # what you target internally (aspirational)
  availability: 99.95%  # tighter than SLA
  latency_p95: 500ms    # tighter than SLA
  # When SLO breaches, you have time to fix before SLA breach

buffer_analysis:
  sla_downtime_per_month: "43.2 minutes"   # 99.9%
  slo_downtime_per_month: "21.6 minutes"   # 99.95%
  early_warning_buffer: "21.6 minutes"     # this is your safety net

items.title: Track SLOs per endpoint, not just per service
items.description: A service-level SLO averages your healthcheck endpoint (always fast) with your report generation endpoint (always slow). The result? Your SLO looks great while your checkout page is on fire. Break SLOs down by endpoint or user journey. The extra work pays off the first time you catch a problem that a service-wide metric would hide.

codeBlocks.label: Per-endpoint SLO tracking
codeBlocks.code:
# Per-endpoint availability
sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="payment-api", endpoint="/api/checkout", status!~"5.."}[30d]))
/ sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="payment-api", endpoint="/api/checkout"}[30d]))

# Per-endpoint latency SLI
histogram_quantile(0.99,
  sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{job="payment-api", endpoint="/api/checkout"}[5m])) by (le)
)

codeBlocks.label: Per-endpoint SLO definitions
codeBlocks.code:
# slo-per-endpoint.yaml
service: payment-api
slos:
  - endpoint: /api/checkout
    availability_target: 99.95%
    latency_p99_target: 800ms
    priority: P0

  - endpoint: /api/products
    availability_target: 99.9%
    latency_p99_target: 200ms
    priority: P1

  - endpoint: /api/admin/reports
    availability_target: 99.5%
    latency_p99_target: 5000ms
    priority: P2
    # Lower target is fine here: internal users,
    # pre-computed data, occasional slow queries expected

items.title: Review and adjust SLOs every quarter
items.description: SLOs are not set-and-forget. Your traffic patterns change, your architecture changes, and your users' expectations change. Review every quarter. If you're always at 100%, your SLO is too loose and you're probably over-investing in reliability. If you're always burning budget, tighten your deployment practices or loosen the target.

codeBlocks.label: Quarterly SLO review queries
codeBlocks.code:
#!/bin/bash
# quarterly-slo-review.sh
# Run this before your quarterly SLO review meeting

PROM="http://prometheus:9090/api/v1/query"
SERVICE="payment-api"

echo "=== Quarterly SLO Review: $SERVICE ==="
echo ""

# 1. Actual availability over the quarter
echo "Availability (last 90 days):"
curl -s "$PROM" --data-urlencode "query=
  sum(rate(http_requests_total{job=\"$SERVICE\", status!~\"5..\"}[90d]))
  / sum(rate(http_requests_total{job=\"$SERVICE\"}[90d]))" \
  | jq -r '.data.result[0].value[1]' | xargs printf "  %.4f%%\n"

# 2. Number of SLO violations
echo "SLO violation events (last 90 days):"
curl -s "$PROM" --data-urlencode "query=
  count_over_time(
    (sum(rate(http_requests_total{job=\"$SERVICE\", status=~\"5..\"}[1h]))
     / sum(rate(http_requests_total{job=\"$SERVICE\"}[1h])) > 0.001)[90d:1h]
  )" | jq -r '.data.result[0].value[1]' | xargs printf "  %s hours\n"

# 3. P99 latency trend
echo "P99 latency (last 90 days):"
curl -s "$PROM" --data-urlencode "query=
  histogram_quantile(0.99,
    sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{job=\"$SERVICE\"}[90d])) by (le)
  )" | jq -r '.data.result[0].value[1]' | xargs printf "  %.3f seconds\n"

echo ""
echo "Questions to answer:"
echo "  - Is the SLO target still right for this service?"
echo "  - Did we spend too much time on reliability vs features?"
echo "  - Are there new user journeys that need their own SLOs?"
